text: The surgeon makes an incision in the right flank, which will later be used for the ileostomy. An 11mm trocar is inserted, followed by the insertion of a 10mm, 30-degree rigid scope.

The surgeon introduces a six millimeter flexible trocar at the twelve o'clock position and another at the six o'clock position. A bi-curved grasping forceps is inserted through the twelve o'clock trocar, while a monocurve grasping forceps is inserted through the six o'clock trocar.

The surgeon places the greater omentum and small bowel above the right liver. A monocurve coagulating hook is inserted at the six o'clock position to identify and dissect the origin of the inferior mesenteric vein. A straight 5mm clipper plier is introduced through the flexible trocar at the same position to clip the vein, followed by monocurve scissors to cut the vein.

The surgeon dissects the left mesocolon, carefully preserving Toldt's fascia. The origin of the inferior mesenteric artery is exposed, clipped with five-millimeter clips, and then cut using monocurve scissors. The dissection of the left mesocolon continues.
The surgeon mobilizes the rectosigmoid colon from medial to lateral, freeing the sigmoid and left colon from the lateral parietal wall.
The surgeon dissects the girotofascia from the left mesocolon. The left mesocolon is then sectioned intercorporeally using a monocurve coagulating hook and secured with five millimeter clips.

The monocurve needle holder is inserted through the flexible trocar at the six o'clock position. A straight needle is introduced percutaneously above the pelvic bone, and the uterus is retracted. The suture is clamped externally using Kelly Graspers.

The surgeon performs a total mesorectal excision, identifying and respecting the presacral fascia. The excision begins with posterior dissection, carefully separating the mesorectal tissue from the surrounding structures.
The surgeon performs lateral dissection to mobilize the left side, followed by anterior mobilization of the rectum while preserving its attachments to the vagina.
The surgeon performs total mesorectal excision (TME) by dissecting downwards and laterally on the left side, then posteriorly while remaining anterior to the presacral fascia.
The levator ani muscle on the left side is clearly visible. A percutaneous suture is introduced and placed into the peritoneal sheath, which covers the vagina.

The surgeon increases the exposure of the operative field and isolates the inferior rectal artery posterior to the rectum. The artery is clipped and divided, completing the total mesorectal excision posteriorly while respecting both levator ani muscles.
The surgeon returns to anterior dissection, mobilizing the rectum from the vagina and laterally. The level for the rectal section is visually determined.

The surgeon introduces a five millimeter scope into the flexible trocar positioned at six o'clock. The eleven millimeter trocar is replaced by a thirteen millimeter trocar to accommodate the linear stapler. The inferior rectum is divided using the linear stapler with a blue load firing.
The surgeon grasps the rectum and removes it through a single axis. The central trocar and the flexible trocar positioned at six o'clock are removed, and the fascia is joined together.

A plastic protection is introduced, and the surgical specimen is extracted. The left colon is transected, and the anvil of the circular stapler is inserted. A purse string suture is performed, securing the anvil, which is then pushed into the abdominal cavity.

The surgeon partially closes the peritoneal sheet and anterior rectifacia. An 11mm trocar is reinserted, and the circular stapler is inserted transanally to perform the colorectal mechanical anastomosis under laparoscopic view.
The surgeon performs a leak test on the colorectal anastomosis. Subsequently, the mesocolic window is closed, beginning with the closure of the pelvic peritoneal sheet.
The surgeon continues the suture until reaching the first arjunal loop, then repositions the entire small bowel back into its natural anatomical position.

The surgeon searches for the ileocecal valve and selects an ileal loop approximately twenty centimeters from it. The trochars are removed, and the selected segment of the small bowel is prepared for a temporary ileostomy at the site of the single incision.
